md: Converting from miles to kilometers, from minutes to seconds, from km/hr to m/s — every one of these problems sounds different, and students often try to memorize a separate rule for each. There's no need. **All unit conversions are just multiplication by $1$, dressed up cleverly.** Once you see the trick, every conversion problem in the world uses the same method.
### Stage 1 — Two true facts, two true fractions
Start with something you already know: $1 \text{ day} = 24 \text{ hours}$. That single fact can be written as two different fractions, and *both of them are equal to $1$*:

Why? Because the *amount* on top and the *amount* on bottom are the same — they just *describe* it in different units. $1$ day IS $24$ hours. It's no different from saying $\dfrac{6}{6} = 1$ or $\dfrac{5280}{5280} = 1$.
**A conversion factor is a fraction whose top and bottom describe the same amount in different units. That makes it equal to $1$.**
### Stage 2 — Multiplying by 1 doesn't change the value, only the form
If you multiply any quantity by a conversion factor (which is just $1$), the quantity stays the same — only how it's *labelled* changes.
$3$ days IS $72$ hours. We didn't *change* how much time we had — we just re-expressed it. Just like $\dfrac{1}{2} = \dfrac{2}{4}$: same amount, different fraction.

blocks.title: The big shift in thinking
blocks.body: Most students think of unit conversion as a separate skill with its own rules. It isn't. It's just multiplication by $1$. Every conversion you'll ever do is this same move.

md: ### Stage 3 — Units cancel like algebra symbols
Here's the second insight. Treat the unit words ($\text{days}$, $\text{hours}$, etc.) like algebra letters that can cancel.
The word "days" appears on top *and* on bottom — it cancels, just like $\dfrac{3x}{x} = 3$. What's left over ("hours") is the unit you wanted.
This cancellation rule does all the work for you. **You don't have to remember whether to multiply or divide — the units tell you.**
### Stage 4 — The "which way goes up?" question answers itself
Students worry: *"do I multiply by $24$ or divide by $24$?"* The factor-label method makes that question disappear. Just follow this rule:
**Put the unit you HAVE on the bottom of the conversion factor (so it cancels), and the unit you WANT on the top (so it survives).**
**Example.** Convert $180$ minutes to hours. You have minutes; you want hours. So put "minutes" on the bottom of the conversion factor:
Minutes cancel, hours survive. The number $180/60 = 3$ comes out automatically. Try it the other way — put "minutes" on top instead:
Nothing cancels, the answer comes out with units of $\dfrac{\text{min}^2}{\text{hr}}$ — clearly wrong. **Wrong units = wrong setup.** That's a built-in error detector.

blocks.title: Your method, in one sentence
blocks.body: Write down what you have. Multiply by conversion factors arranged so the unwanted units cancel and the wanted units survive. The arithmetic falls out automatically.

md: ### Stage 5 — Chaining: many 1's at once
What if there's no direct "seconds to days" conversion factor handy? No problem — just multiply by *several* $1$'s in a row, each one a step in a chain.
**Convert $180$ seconds to days.** We don't know seconds-to-days directly, but we know seconds-to-minutes, minutes-to-hours, and hours-to-days. Stack them:
Every middle unit (sec, min, hr) appears once on top and once on bottom — they all cancel. The only surviving unit is "day". The number works out to:
Same method as a single conversion — just more $1$'s strung together. As long as the units cancel cleanly all the way through, the chain is correct.
### Bonus — compound units like km/hr → m/s

Conversion factors can stack to handle units that involve *both* a top and a bottom (speed, density, currency-per-time, etc.). To convert $90$ km/hr to m/s, we need to convert km → m on the top *and* hr → s on the bottom. So we use two conversion factors:
$\text{km}$ cancels (top–bottom), $\text{hr}$ cancels (top–bottom). Left over: $\text{m}$ on top, $\text{s}$ on bottom — exactly what we wanted. Compute:

So $90$ km/hr $= 25$ m/s. Same method, two factors. Once you've internalized "multiply by $1$, let units cancel," every conversion problem in physics, chemistry, engineering, and everyday life uses this same recipe.

blocks.title: Useful conversion facts to keep handy
blocks.body: $1 \text{ km} = 1000 \text{ m}$. $1 \text{ m} = 100 \text{ cm}$. $1 \text{ hr} = 60 \text{ min} = 3600 \text{ s}$. $1 \text{ day} = 24 \text{ hr}$. $1 \text{ mile} \approx 1.609 \text{ km}$. $1 \text{ kg} = 1000 \text{ g}$. $1 \text{ L} = 1000 \text{ mL}$. Each of these gives you TWO conversion factors — one with the bigger unit on top, one with it on bottom. Pick whichever makes your units cancel.

md: ### Quick mental checklist before every problem
1. **What do I have?** Quantity and units.
2. **What do I want?** Quantity and units (or units only, for proportional problems).
3. **What's known equal?** Pick conversion facts that link the two.
4. **Set up so units cancel.** Have-units on the bottom; want-units on the top.
5. **Multiply through.** The arithmetic does the rest.
6. **Sanity check.** Did the right units survive? If yes, the answer is at least dimensionally correct.
